examples.title: Pseudocode: Advanced Linked List Operations
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating concatenation, reversal, cycle detection, middle element finding, and merging sorted lists
examples.code:
ALGORITHM ConcatenateLists(list1, list2)
INPUT: Two linked lists list1 and list2
OUTPUT: Concatenated linked list

BEGIN
 IF list1 == NULL THEN
 RETURN list2
 END IF
 IF list2 == NULL THEN
 RETURN list1
 END IF
 
 temp ← list1
 WHILE temp.next != NULL DO
 temp ← temp.next
 END WHILE
 temp.next ← list2
 RETURN list1
END

ALGORITHM ReverseList(head)
INPUT: Head of linked list
OUTPUT: Head of reversed linked list

BEGIN
 prev ← NULL
 current ← head
 next ← NULL
 
 WHILE current != NULL DO
 next ← current.next
 current.next ← prev
 prev ← current
 current ← next
 END WHILE
 RETURN prev
END

ALGORITHM DetectCycle(head)
INPUT: Head of linked list
OUTPUT: Boolean indicating if cycle exists

BEGIN
 IF head == NULL THEN
 RETURN FALSE
 END IF
 
 slow ← head
 fast ← head
 
 WHILE fast != NULL AND fast.next != NULL DO
 slow ← slow.next
 fast ← fast.next.next
 IF slow == fast THEN
 RETURN TRUE
 END IF
 END WHILE
 RETURN FALSE
END

ALGORITHM FindMiddle(head)
INPUT: Head of linked list
OUTPUT: Middle node of linked list

BEGIN
 IF head == NULL THEN
 RETURN NULL
 END IF
 
 slow ← head
 fast ← head
 
 WHILE fast != NULL AND fast.next != NULL DO
 slow ← slow.next
 fast ← fast.next.next
 END WHILE
 RETURN slow
END

ALGORITHM MergeSortedLists(list1, list2)
INPUT: Two sorted linked lists
OUTPUT: Merged sorted linked list

BEGIN
 dummy ← Node with data 0
 tail ← dummy
 
 WHILE list1 != NULL AND list2 != NULL DO
 IF list1.data <= list2.data THEN
 tail.next ← list1
 list1 ← list1.next
 ELSE
 tail.next ← list2
 list2 ← list2.next
 END IF
 tail ← tail.next
 END WHILE
 
 IF list1 != NULL THEN
 tail.next ← list1
 END IF
 IF list2 != NULL THEN
 tail.next ← list2
 END IF
 
 RETURN dummy.next
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode covers five fundamental advanced linked list operations: 1) Concatenation appends list2 to the end of list1 by traversing to the last node. 2) Reversal uses three pointers (prev, current, next) to reverse links in-place. 3) Cycle detection uses Floyd's Tortoise and Hare algorithm with slow/fast pointers. 4) Finding middle uses the same two-pointer technique. 5) MergeSortedLists combines two sorted lists using a dummy node approach.

examples.title: Python: Advanced Linked List Operations Implementation
examples.description: Complete Python implementation of all advanced linked list operations with classes and functions
examples.code:
class Node:
 def __init__(self, data):
 self.data = data
 self.next = None

class LinkedList:
 def __init__(self):
 self.head = None
 
 def append(self, data):
 new_node = Node(data)
 if not self.head:
 self.head = new_node
 return new_node
 current = self.head
 while current.next:
 current = current.next
 current.next = new_node
 return new_node
 
 def display(self):
 elements = []
 current = self.head
 while current:
 elements.append(str(current.data))
 current = current.next
 elements.append("NULL")
 print(" → ".join(elements))

def concatenate_lists(list1, list2):
 """Concatenate list2 to the end of list1"""
 if list1.head is None:
 return list2
 if list2.head is None:
 return list1
 
 current = list1.head
 while current.next:
 current = current.next
 current.next = list2.head
 return list1

def reverse_list(head):
 """Reverse linked list in-place using three pointers"""
 prev = None
 current = head
 
 while current:
 next_node = current.next
 current.next = prev
 prev = current
 current = next_node
 
 return prev

def detect_cycle(head):
 """Floyd's Cycle Detection Algorithm (Tortoise and Hare)"""
 if head is None:
 return False
 
 slow = head
 fast = head
 
 while fast and fast.next:
 slow = slow.next
 fast = fast.next.next
 
 if slow == fast:
 return True
 
 return False

def find_middle(head):
 """Find middle element using two-pointer technique"""
 if head is None:
 return None
 
 slow = head
 fast = head
 
 while fast and fast.next:
 slow = slow.next
 fast = fast.next.next
 
 return slow

def merge_sorted_lists(list1, list2):
 """Merge two sorted linked lists"""
 dummy = Node(0)
 tail = dummy
 
 while list1 and list2:
 if list1.data <= list2.data:
 tail.next = list1
 list1 = list1.next
 else:
 tail.next = list2
 list2 = list2.next
 tail = tail.next
 
 tail.next = list1 if list1 else list2
 return dummy.next

def merge_sort_linked_list(head):
 """Merge sort for linked lists"""
 if head is None or head.next is None:
 return head
 
 middle = find_middle(head)
 next_of_middle = middle.next
 middle.next = None
 
 left = merge_sort_linked_list(head)
 right = merge_sort_linked_list(next_of_middle)
 
 return merge_sorted_lists(left, right)

# Demonstration
if __name__ == "__main__":
 # Test Concatenation
 list1 = LinkedList()
 list2 = LinkedList()
 
 for x in [1, 2, 3]:
 list1.append(x)
 for x in [4, 5, 6]:
 list2.append(x)
 
 print("List 1:")
 list1.display()
 print("List 2:")
 list2.display()
 
 concatenate_lists(list1, list2)
 print("After concatenation:")
 list1.display()
 
 # Test Reversal
 rev_head = reverse_list(list1.head)
 list1.head = rev_head
 print("After reversal:")
 list1.display()
 
 # Test Find Middle
 test_list = LinkedList()
 for x in [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]:
 test_list.append(x)
 middle = find_middle(test_list.head)
 print(f"Middle element: {middle.data if middle else 'None'}")
 
 # Test Merge Sorted Lists
 sorted1 = LinkedList()
 sorted2 = LinkedList()
 
 for x in [1, 3, 5]:
 sorted1.append(x)
 for x in [2, 4, 6]:
 sorted2.append(x)
 
 merged_head = merge_sorted_lists(sorted1.head, sorted2.head)
 print("Merged sorted list:")
 temp = merged_head
 while temp:
 print(temp.data, end=" → " if temp.next else "")
 temp = temp.next
 print(" → NULL")
 
 # Test Cycle Detection
 cycle_test = LinkedList()
 for x in range(1, 5):
 cycle_test.append(x)
 print(f"\nCycle detected: {detect_cycle(cycle_test.head)}")
examples.explanation: This Python implementation demonstrates all major advanced linked list operations: concatenation joins two lists, reversal uses iterative pointer manipulation, cycle detection uses Floyd's algorithm with slow/fast pointers, find_middle uses the same technique, merge_sorted_lists combines sorted lists with a dummy node, and merge_sort implements divide-and-conquer sorting. The code includes a complete LinkedList class with helper methods and comprehensive test cases.
examples.timeComplexity: Concatenation: O(n), Reversal: O(n), Cycle Detection: O(n), Find Middle: O(n), Merge: O(n+m), Merge Sort: O(n log n)
examples.spaceComplexity: Concatenation: O(1), Reversal: O(1), Cycle Detection: O(1), Find Middle: O(1), Merge: O(1), Merge Sort: O(log n)

examples.title: C: Advanced Linked List Operations Implementation
examples.description: Complete C implementation of advanced linked list operations with structs and functions
examples.explanation: This C implementation provides complete implementations of all advanced linked list operations: concatenate() joins two lists, reverseList() performs in-place reversal using three pointers, hasCycle() implements Floyd's Tortoise and Hare algorithm for cycle detection, findMiddle() uses the two-pointer technique, mergeSortedLists() combines sorted lists with a dummy node, and mergeSort() implements divide-and-conquer merge sort. The code includes proper memory allocation with malloc, comprehensive test cases in main(), and clear comments explaining each operation.
examples.timeComplexity: Concatenation: O(n), Reversal: O(n), Cycle Detection: O(n), Find Middle: O(n), Merge: O(n+m), Merge Sort: O(n log n)
examples.spaceComplexity: Concatenation: O(1), Reversal: O(1), Cycle Detection: O(1), Find Middle: O(1), Merge: O(1), Merge Sort: O(log n)

examples.title: Java: Advanced Linked List Operations Implementation
examples.description: Complete Java implementation with LinkedList class and all advanced operations
examples.explanation: This Java implementation provides a generic, type-safe LinkedList class with all advanced operations: concatenate() joins two lists, reverseList() performs in-place reversal, hasCycle() implements Floyd's algorithm, findMiddle() uses two-pointer technique, mergeSortedLists() combines sorted lists, and mergeSort() implements divide-and-conquer sorting. The implementation uses generics (Comparable for sorted operations), includes proper encapsulation with private fields, and provides comprehensive test cases demonstrating all operations in the main method.
examples.timeComplexity: Concatenation: O(n), Reversal: O(n), Cycle Detection: O(n), Find Middle: O(n), Merge: O(n+m), Merge Sort: O(n log n)
examples.spaceComplexity: Concatenation: O(1), Reversal: O(1), Cycle Detection: O(1), Find Middle: O(1), Merge: O(1), Merge Sort: O(log n)
